question: What makes up living soil?
title: What Makes Up Living Soil? Inside the Web of Life Beneath Your Feet
metaDescription: Living soil isn't dirt — it's a thriving ecosystem of bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and more. Scotty breaks down the soil food web and why it's the foundation of everything you grow.
content:
I get asked this question all the time. What is living soil? How can you tell if your soil is living? And the easiest way I know to answer it is to pick up a handful of good, biologically active soil and show you the difference between that and a handful of dirt.

Dirt is mineral particles. Sand, silt, clay. It holds moisture at varying rates, it compacts under pressure, and it doesn't do much else on its own. You can grow things in it with enough synthetic fertilizer, but you're doing all the work yourself. You're the chemistry department.

Living soil is a different thing entirely. Pick up a scoop of genuinely healthy living soil and it holds its structure — it crumbles but doesn't fall apart, it has texture, it has what soil scientists call aggregate structure. Those aggregates, those little clumps of soil that hold together even when the soil is moist, are held together by a light biological glue. Specifically, bacterial biofilms and fungal hyphae are cementing those particles together. You are holding evidence of biological activity in your hand.

That's where the story starts.

---

## The Organisms That Make Soil Live

The USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer is one of the best resources I know on this subject, and it describes the soil food web as a network of organisms including plants, bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, and larger predatory species — all interacting in one of the most complex ecosystems on Earth.

Let me walk through the main players.

**Bacteria** are the smallest organisms in the soil and they are everywhere. In a single teaspoon of healthy soil, there can be hundreds of millions to billions of individual bacteria representing thousands of different species. They are the primary decomposers of fresh organic matter. They fix nitrogen from the atmosphere — taking that N2 gas that makes up 78 percent of the air and converting it into a form plants can actually use. They produce enzymes that break down complex organic compounds into simpler ones. They are the starting point for most nutrient cycles in the soil.

**Fungi** are the second major player, and in many healthy soil systems they actually dominate the biomass even though their individual numbers are lower. The hyphae — those thin white threads you see running through good compost or through forest duff — are fungal filaments, and they extend through the soil like a fiber-optic network. Mycorrhizal fungi form direct partnerships with plant roots, extending the plant's effective root system by orders of magnitude and trading minerals (especially phosphorus) for sugars that the plant produces through photosynthesis. One teaspoon of healthy forest soil can contain several miles of fungal hyphae.

**Protozoa** are single-celled organisms that graze on bacteria. This sounds minor but it is critical. When a protozoan eats bacteria, it ingests more nitrogen than it needs and excretes the excess as plant-available ammonium. This is called the microbial loop, and it is one of the primary mechanisms by which nitrogen becomes available to plant roots. Healthy protozoan populations mean healthy nitrogen cycling.

**Nematodes** are microscopic roundworms, and like protozoa they exist in predator-prey relationships within the soil food web. Bacterial-feeding nematodes graze on bacteria. Fungal-feeding nematodes graze on fungal hyphae. Predatory nematodes eat other nematodes and protozoa. Each of these predation events releases nutrients that cycle back to plants.

**Arthropods and earthworms** are the larger macroorganisms — the ones you can actually see. Earthworms pull organic matter down into the soil, mix it with mineral particles, and produce castings that are extraordinarily nutrient-rich and biologically active. A good population of earthworms is one of the best indicators of soil health you can observe without a microscope. Arthropods — mites, springtails, beetles — shred organic matter into smaller pieces, making it accessible to the bacterial and fungal communities.

## How to Tell If Your Soil Is Living

Here's what I look for in the field.

Aggregates. If I pick up a handful of moist soil and it crumbles into small, irregular clumps that hold their shape rather than smearing or falling apart into individual particles, those aggregates are being held together by biological activity. USDA NRCS describes aggregate formation as one of the key biological indicators of soil health. Good aggregates mean good pore structure — which means good water infiltration, good aeration, good root penetration.

Earthworms. Dig down six inches in genuinely living soil and you should encounter earthworms. In a healthy system you might find dozens per square foot. If you dig down and find nothing — no worms, no insects, no biological activity — that soil is depleted.

Fungal hyphae. Break apart a chunk of good soil or a piece of partially decomposed mulch and you should see white or off-white threads running through it. That's fungal hyphae. That's a sign the fungal network is intact and active.

The smell. Living soil smells like living soil. That earthy, rich, almost sweet smell — geosmin produced by actinomycetes — is one of the most reliable sensory indicators of biological activity. Depleted, minerally exhausted soil smells like nothing, or like dust.

## The Decay Cycle and Why It Matters

Albert Howard understood something at Indore in the 1930s that the rest of the agricultural world has been slowly catching up to ever since. The decay cycle — the process by which organic matter is broken down and rebuilt into stable humus — is not a side process. It is the central process. It is what makes soil alive, what makes plants healthy, what makes nutrients available, what makes a farm or garden function without chemical inputs.

Gabe Brown builds on this directly in his work on regenerative agriculture. You cannot have a healthy farm without a healthy soil food web. You cannot have a healthy soil food web without organic matter. You cannot have organic matter without something covering and feeding the soil continuously.

The life in the soil is fed by two things: the organic matter you add from the surface (mulch, compost, cover crop residue), and the sugars that plant roots exude into the rhizosphere to feed specific microbial populations. That second one is critical. A living plant is actively farming its own microbial community around its roots. It produces and releases specific sugars to attract and feed the specific organisms it needs for nutrient exchange. This is a 400-million-year-old relationship and it is still operating in every living plant root zone on Earth.

When you kill the soil biology — through tillage, through synthetic inputs that bypass the microbial pathway, through leaving soil bare and exposed to sun and wind — you break that relationship. You have dirt with chemistry applied to it. You don't have living soil.

Living soil is what y'all are building when you compost, when you mulch, when you grow cover crops, when you minimize disturbance. Every one of those practices feeds the web. And the web feeds everything above it.

That's what makes soil live.

---

## Sources

1. USDA NRCS — Soil Biology Primer: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/resources/education-and-teaching-materials/soil-biology-primer
2. USDA NRCS — Healthy Soils Are Full of Life: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/sites/default/files/2023-01/Healthy-Soils-Are-full-of-life.pdf
3. USDA NRCS — Soil Health: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/conservation-basics/natural-resource-concerns/soil/soil-health
4. Ohio State University Extension — Understanding Soil Microbes and Nutrient Recycling: https://ohioline.osu.edu/factsheet/SAG-16
5. NC State Extension — Soil Biological Health: https://soilmanagement.ces.ncsu.edu/soil-health/soil-biological-health/
6. USDA NRCS — Biological Indicators and Soil Functions: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/sites/default/files/2023-01/Indicators-Biological-Indicators-and-Soil-Functions.pdf

sources.title: Healthy Soils Are Full of Life
Understanding Soil Microbes and Nutrient Recycling

scottyQuotes: There is a life web within the soil. It starts with bacteria and fungi and all these very small organisms that are living within the soil.
Living microbe-rich soil will have aggregates — this is a light glue layer that holds soil particles together.
The easiest way to show this is to find an aggregate and point out the difference.
